coredata.dc:description: Hybrid super capacitors are gaining popularity due to their enormous potential for energy-storing applications. A simple hydrothermal process was utilized for the synthesis of nickel strontium sulfide (NiSrS) and then graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were incorporated in different ratios to find out the optimized wt% ratio. An optimal combination of GQDs with NiSrS improved conductivity by producing higher active sites, which aids in modifying how the composite behaves electrochemically. The prepared NiSrS/GQDs-5% composite displayed an outstanding specific capacitance of 1881 C/g at 2.0 A/g. The developed aqueous-based anti-symmetric supercapattery device had an outstanding energy density of 44 Wh/kg at a power density of 1750 W/kg with remarkable capacitance retention of 98.3 % and columbic efficiency of 100 %. The NiSrS/NGQDs-5% demonstrated a low 87 mV during HER test. The exceptional electrochemical properties of GQDs-induced NiSrS pave the way for constructing highly effective electrode materials, making them ideal for supercapacitor applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hybrid super capacitors are gaining popularity due to their enormous potential for energy-storing applications. A simple hydrothermal process was utilized for the synthesis of nickel strontium sulfide (NiSrS) and then graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were incorporated in different ratios to find out the optimized wt% ratio. An optimal combination of GQDs with NiSrS improved conductivity by producing higher active sites, which aids in modifying how the composite behaves electrochemically. The prepared NiSrS/GQDs-5% composite displayed an outstanding specific capacitance of 1881 C/g at 2.0 A/g. The developed aqueous-based anti-symmetric supercapattery device had an outstanding energy density of 44 Wh/kg at a power density of 1750 W/kg with remarkable capacitance retention of 98.3 % and columbic efficiency of 100 %. The NiSrS/NGQDs-5% demonstrated a low 87 mV during HER test. The exceptional electrochemical properties of GQDs-induced NiSrS pave the way for constructing highly effective electrode materials, making them ideal for supercapacitor applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors (SCs) are distinguishing themselves as the best alternative for energy storage devices since they possess good energy storage along maintaining their power density. Particularly, the SCs display great life-span in cyclic operations, quick charging and discharging, exceptional reversibility, and incredible power density values [1,2].

Water electrolysis as a hydrogen synthesis process offers a promising option to store energy produced from renewable sources such as solar energy [3–7]. Many solar water-splitting systems operate in acidic electrolytes, using high-quality hydrogen catalysts primarily made of precious metals such as platinum (Pt). However, the limited supply of Pt limits its global application, making it unworkable [8]. As a result, the search for economical, acid-stable replacements capable of aiding water splitting becomes even more appealing. Numerous examples have surfaced, indicating the viability of materials such as MoS2 [9], MoSe2 [10], WS2 [11], WSe2 [12], MoB [13], Mo2C [14], NiMoNx [15], and Ni2P [16].

A critical factor that has a significant impact on SCs performance is the careful selection of suitable electrode materials. These substances cover a variety of possibilities, such as metal oxides, sulfides, and phosphates. The total effectiveness and efficiency of SCs are greatly influenced by the material used for the electrodes. Unique characteristics of various materials have an impact on critical elements like energy storage capability, cyclical equilibrium, and charge/discharge rates. Because it directly affects their performance and decides whether they are suitable for a variety of energy storage applications, a selection of electrode materials is, therefore, a crucial factor in the design and optimization development of super capacitors [17]. Metal sulfide (MSs) shows a very interesting contender for electrode material in SCs application due to its exceptional qualities. MSs has a substantial theoretical capacitance, which enables it to store a large quantity of energy [18–20]. The nickel sulfide has exceptional redox reversibility, enabling effective both charging and discharging cycles. Additionally, nickel sulfide is environmentally favorable, which fits with the expanding demand for renewable energy preservation technologies. Nevertheless, despite these outstanding characteristics, there are certain difficulties in actually using nickel sulfides in SCs. One such barrier is the material has weak electrical conductivity, which reduces its overall performance. As a result, for nickel sulfides, the specific capacitance is smaller than what is predicted theoretically. The extensive use of nickel sulfides in realistic storage mechanisms for energy is restricted by these drawbacks. Strontium sulfide, an underexplored material in the context of SCs utilization, has recently garnered scientific interest. This is primarily due to its untapped potential for yielding superior efficiencies, hinging on its ability to manifest exceptional conductivity and forge innovative nanostructures. These attributes hold the promise of bolstering the effective surface area, a coveted trait in the realm of SCs technology. A comprehensive research on overcoming these obstacles and developing methods to increase the electrical conductivity, and cycle stability is required [21,22]. Researchers have developed an approach of incorporating the use of conductive substrates to address the issues caused by the poor electrical conductivity of nickel sulfides. Scientists are able to directly synthesized composite materials including nickel sulfides by using substrates including nickel foam, carbon fabric, or metal sheets. With this method, the electrical conductivity of the electrodes for super capacitor can be improved. The conductive substrates act as a base or framework on which to deposit or grow the composite material. Due to an effective electron transport made possible by this configuration, the nickel sulfide inherently low conductivity is overridden. In order to promote electrical communication throughout the electrode material, the conductive substrates produce a network of channels for electron transport. Because of their superior electrical conductivity qualities, nickel foam, carbon fabric, and metal sheets were chosen. As a result of their enormous surface area, the combination of composite material and conductive substrate can make more contact with one another. This better contact promotes increased overall conductivity and guarantees effective charge transfer [23–25]. Adding metals with better electrical conductivities to the NiS structure is an alternative strategy for the enhancement of conductivity due to rich active sites [26,27]. The addition of carbonaceous materials regulate their interiors during protracted cycle stability tests in addition to stabilizing the electrode materials [28–33]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used largely in a number of domains, including detectors, luminescence, optical visualization detectors, and applications involving the storage of energy. Similar to graphene, these GQDs have sp2 hybridized carbon, however, they have a 0D structure and a diameter between 1 and 20 nm. Enhanced particular surface areas, variable band gaps, and the capacity to be doped with various heteroatoms are just a few of their outstanding properties. GQDs thus provide a possible route for the improvement of electrochemical characteristics [34–36]. Recent research has investigated the effects of adding GQDs to various metal oxide/sulfide materials for super capacitor applications [37,38]. When nickel and strontium sulfides are mixed, they may display richer redox chemistry than when they are used separately and have a larger theoretical capacity.

The impact of adding GQDs to nickel strontium sulfide (NiSrS) is discussed in this study. We carried out a straightforward hydrothermal synthesis to produce NiSrS-GQDs. The NiSrS/GQDs served as a binder-free electrode in SCs. The structural analyses were done by using the x-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Raman spectrum. The electrochemical performance of the prepared NiSrS and NiSrS-GQD composites is evaluated using cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanic charging and discharging (GCD). The cyclic performance of NiSrS-GQD was determined by measuring capacity retention after 10,000 cycles.

The nickel foam used in this experiment came from MTI Corp. in the USA. The materials, which included citric acid (C6H8O7), thiourea (CH4N2S), strontium nitrate (Sr(NO3)2, nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2, and urea (CH4N2O), were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Hychem Laboratories supplied the ethanol and hydrochloric acid, which were used exactly as they were delivered.

A procedure was used to eliminate the oxide layer from the outermost layer of the nickel foam that was produced. First, a 30-mins ultrasonic treatment was performed on the nickel foam. A solution of 3 M hydrochloric acid (HCl) was used for this purpose. The remaining acid was then carefully washed out of the foam using deionized water (DIW). To ensure complete cleaning, a total of three ethanol washes were also performed after that. In order to thoroughly remove any remaining moisture, the nickel foam was vacuum dried out for 8 h at a temperature of 60 °C.

The hydrothermal process was used to produce GQDs. There were various steps in the process. In the beginning, 10 ml of DIW was utilized to dissolve 360 mg of urea (CH4N2O) and 420 mg of citric acid (C3H4O3). The mixture was continually stirred to produce a uniform, well-mixed solution. The solution was then put into a 50 ml autoclave and retained for 4 h at a temperature of 160
°
C. The autoclave was then permitted to cool down at room temperature. Centrifugation was used to remove the resultant GQDs from the solution, and they were then dried at 80 °C. Due to further use or analysis, this drying procedure made sure that the GQDs were free of any remaining moisture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NiSrS and NiSrS-GQD compounds
ce:section.ce:para: 0.2 M of thiourea (CH4N2S) were mixed with 0.5 M of Sr(NO3)2 in a 50 mL of DIW to produce a mix solution. After stirring for about 30 min, the resultant solution was further incorporated with acetic acid (CH2O2) at a concentration of 0.7 M. The mixture was thoroughly combined before being transported to an autoclave of Teflon-coated stainless steel, heated for 8 h at 140 °C. After that, it was left to cool down to room temperature. The resultant SrS powder was collected and put through many ethanol and DIW washes. The SrS material was then dried for an additional 8 h at room temperature. The SrS material was then electrochemically deposited on nickel foam (NF) to serve as the working electrode. The NiS synthesis includes the addition 0.2 M of thiourea (CH4N2S) and 0.5 M Ni (NO3)2.6H2O to form a homogenous solution. The previous steps are followed to synthesize the process. In the synthesis of NiSrS, 0.25 M of Sr (NO3)2, 0.25 M of Ni (NO3)2 and 0.2 M of thiourea (CH4N2S) were mixed together to form a homogeneous solution. Again the above process was followed to obtain NiSrS. The electrochemical performance of individual NiS and SrS were discussed in the supplementary section. Fig. 1 provides an illustration of the synthesis process. NiSrS and a particular quantity (3 %, 5 %, and 10 %) of GQDs were first combined in 50 ml of DIW to produce a solution. And after then similar steps were followed to obtain NiSrS/GQDs-3% (NSG-1), NiSrS/GQDs-5% (NSG-2), and NiSrS/GQDs-10 % (NSG-3).

X-ray diffraction (XRD- Bruker D8) with Cu–K radiation (λ = 1.5406) was used to analyze the crystal structure of the NiSrS (Strontium nickel Sulfide) and NiSrS/GQDs composites. A 532 nm laser-equipped Witec-Alpha 300 RAMAN system was used for Raman characterization. FESEM (Field-Emission Scanning Electron Microscope) with a Hitachi-SU3500 model were both utilized to examine the outer layer microstructure of the processed NiSrS and NiSrS/GQD composites. A Micromeritics ASAP 2020 analyzer was used to perform the BET (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) to calculate the precise surface area. The multi-point BET equation was utilized with the received data to estimate the particular surface area.

The electrochemical analyses were done utilizing a 1 M KOH electrolyte and a conventional three-electrode configuration with an CS-300 workstation. The working electrode without a binder was made of NiSrS and NiSrS/GQD composites on Ni foams. A Pt/wire of 1 cm2 served as the counter electrode, and an Hg/HgO electrode in a KOH solution served as the reference electrode. We mixed 2.85 g of KOH crystals with 50 mL of DIW to produce a solution of 1 M that transfer charges between the electrodes. The cleaned nickel foam was then covered in the slurry, which area has
(
3 × 1
)
c
m 2
. We put 5–6 mg of the substance on the nickel foam. Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were carried out on the produced composites to evaluate their electrochemical performance. The GCD testing was conducted within the current density range of 2.0–3.6 A/g, while the CV tests were performed from 3 to 50 mV/s scan rate. These tests gave us important information about the both composite electrochemical behavior. Through repeated GCD experiments with a current density of 8 A/g, both composite cyclic stability was assessed in order to determine its capacity to maintain constant electrochemical performance over time. Additionally, at the open circuit potential, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) investigation was carried out in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz–100 kHz. Subsequently, asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was constructed using activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode and NiSrS/GQDs as the positive electrode. The performance of this ASC device was determined using electrochemical testing in a 1 M KOH electrolyte. The galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves were used to calculate the specific capacity, termed as
C s
(measured in columbe per gram, C/g). Using Equation (1), which reads as follows,

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns obtained from the as-prepared nanostructured NiSrS/GQDs samples are shown in Fig. 2 (a). The results of the research showed that the patterns are consistent with a NiS rhombohedral structure that belongs to the R3m space group with a = 9.61 Å and c = 3.16 Å. associate with JCPDS card No. 01-073-1178 [46]. The XRD spike at 25° belongs to GQDs plane (002) [47]. The particle size for NiSrS and NiSrS/GQDs composites were measures using the Scherrer formula [48,49]:
(5)

Where D is the average crystallite size. K is a dimensionless shape factor, typically around 0.9. λ is the wavelength of the X-rays used (usually provided by the X-ray source, such as copper Kα radiation with a wavelength of 1.5406 Å). β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the X-ray diffraction peak. θ is the Bragg angle, which is the angle at which the X-rays are diffracted by the crystal lattice. The particle size for NiSrS, NiSrS/GQDs-3% (NSG-1), NiSrS/GQDs-5% (NSG-2), and NiSrS/GQDs-10 % (NSG-3) were 0.68, 0.65, 0.69, and 0.67 nm, respectively.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image provided valuable insights into the size and shape of the NiSrS/GQDs nanoparticles. In Fig. 2(b), the formation of GQDs on NiSrS was observed in the form of a well-defined thin film. These nanoparticles displayed a distinct diameter, measuring just a few nanometers. The chemical process that led to the development of GQDs on NiSrS nanoparticles unveiled the crucial role played by the GQD thin film, in facilitating the transportation of active sites that initiate the growth of these nanoparticles.

The UV–vis spectrum obtained for NiS, SrS, NiSrS, and NiSrS/GQDs was depicted in Fig. 2(c). The two minor peaks were noticed around
226
n m
and
272
n m
in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the NiS hierarchical nanostructures, which added to the intriguing findings. These results align well with previous findings reported by Salavati-Niasari et al. [50]. Notably, the absorption spectrum exhibited a noticeable blue shift when compared to that of bulk NiS, indicating a lower energy bandgap of approximately 2.3 eV.

The SrS showed a distinct peak is observed at around 427 nm, which can be explained by direct absorption in the dopant levels [51]. It is important to note that SrS does not emit light without an activator due to its nature as an indirect band gap semiconductor. It is well known that GQDs show optical absorption, mainly in the ultraviolet (UV) range but also extending into the visible spectrum. No distinctive features are seen, which is consistent with earlier research on GQDs, with the exception of a peak about 210 nm caused by an aromatic CC bond
π
-to-
π
* transition [50].

At room temperature, the Raman spectra of NiS, SrS, and NiSrS were shown in Fig. 2(d), were produced. The NiS exhibits five distinct vibrational modes at frequencies of 145, 255, 310, 361, and 383 cm−1, which is consistent with the past work [52,53]. Some vibrational modes, though, went undetected. The vibrations within the crystal lattice have a significant impact on Raman vibrational modes; this might be attributable to the size effect of the nanomaterial. When crystals are formed at the nanoscale, their atomic arrangements may vary from those of bulk materials, which could affect the vibrational modes that are recorded. There are several distinctive peaks that can be observed in the SrS Raman spectrum. The stretching vibration of the S–S bonds in SrS is often linked to this peak that is around 198 cm−1 in height. In SrS symmetric stretching mode this peak is attributed to the S–S bonds that 360 cm−1 maximum height. The bending mode of the S–S bonds in SrS is represented by this peak of about 458 cm−1. This peak has to do with the Sr–S bonds' stretching vibration in SrS of around 580 cm−1 [54]. Fig. S1 represents the Raman spectrum for GQDs.

The chemical state and elemental composition of the NiSrS/GQDs composite powders were studied by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Fig. 3 ). The Ni 2p spectra have two strong peaks: Ni 2p3/2 at 856.4 eV and Ni 2p1/2 at 874.3 eV binding energy (Fig. 3(a)). Two peaks with binding energies of 132.3 eV and 135.6 eV compositions were due to the Sr 3d5/2 and Sr 3d3/2 orbitals, respectively (Fig. 3(b)). This doublet signal presence suggests that the Sr atom and the matrix of the perovskite structure have formed a chemical bond. Thedeconvoluted XPS spectrum for S 2p portion had two peaks that spectra had maxima at energies equivalent to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2, respectively, at 166.7 eV and 161.5 eV (Fig. 3(c)) [55,56]. The sulfur species that are present in the composite are revealed by these peaks. These results aid in the interpretation of the properties and prospective uses of the NiSrS/GQDs composite by providing a thorough understanding of the composition and structure of the material. Fig. S2 represents the XPS survey spectrum for GQDs. The deconvoluted XPS spectrum for GQDs indicates the presence of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s.

The C1s high-resolution XPS spectrum reveals the detection of three distinct types of C atoms: graphitic C–C and CC bonds at 282.4 eV, C–O functionalities at 285.8 eV, and
C = O
and
C
O 2
H
groups at 289.3 eV (Fig. 3(d)). However, it is worth noting that the latter two functionalities may be present in GQDs due to the small energy difference between
C − C
or
C = C
and
C − N
or CN functionalities [65]. Fig. 3(e) exhibits two N 1s signals located at 398.3 eV (representing
C − N
and
C = N
bonds) and at 403.34 eV (indicative of protonated
N
H
3 +
functionalities attached to carbon atoms), thereby providing further evidence of the covalent anchorage of 2,2’-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) onto the GQD surface through either C–N or CN bonds. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum demonstrates a single component at 533.2 eV, corresponding to the C–O and CO functionalities (Fig. 3(f)).

The BET measurements were utilized to compute the specific surface area (SSA) of NiSrS and its composites with GQDs. Fig. 4 displays the results of the BET tests. It is apparent the SSA of NiSrS without GQD addition was only 123 m2/g. The SSA of the resultant composites increases with GQD doping amount in the composites increased, maximum at about 5 wt% GQD content and subsequently decreasing. The composites having 5 wt% GQD had an incredible BET-specific surface area of 238.19 m2/g, outperforming both NiSrS/GQD-3% (183.31 m2/g) and pure NSG-3(208.34 m2/g). This substantial increase in the specific surface area demonstrates that including GQD nanoparticles of NiSrS decreases layer-to-layer stacking when compared to pure NiSrS. However, as the GQD level reached 10 % or more, the corresponding composites experienced a slight reduction in BET surface area, though it maintained far larger than that of pure NiSrS. This drop could be associated with GQDs nanoparticles accumulate at higher concentrations, limiting their ability to restrict NiSrS restacking. Different key parameters such as SSA, pore size, and volume for all NiSrS, NiSrS/GQDs-3%, NiSrS/GQDs-5%, and NSG-3were shown in Table 1 .

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and galvanostatic charging and discharging (GCD) were performed using the Corrtest CS300 Potentiostat to investigate electrochemical characteristics of the NiSrS, and NiSrS/GQDs composites.

An electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test was performed to obtain a more comprehensive understanding of the composite intrinsic electrochemical performance. Fig. 5 illustrates the Nyquist schemes that NiSrS, NiSrS/GQD3%, NiSrS/GQD-5%, and NiSrS/GQD-10 % electrodes over a frequency range of 100 kHz - 0.01 Hz, and inset demonstrate corresponding fitting circuit. At higher frequencies, the imperfect half-circle region reflects the charge transfer resistance (Rct), which arises at the interface between the electrode and the electrolyte during the electrochemical process. The Rct value of the NiSrS/GQD-5% composite is much lower than that of the initial NiSrS electrode. The reduction in Rct can be due to the inserting of GQDs into the NiSrS structure, which improves the composite conductivity as previously explained. Huang et al. [57] observed similar results for GQD/CuCo2S4, where the addition of GQDs resulted in decreased Rct values when compared to pure CuCo2S4. GQDs increased conductivity helps to reduce the overall resistance of the composite. Enhancing synergy effects from various elements significantly improved the electrochemical stability of the composite. The NSG-2composite had a high-conductivity as compared to NSG-1and NiSrS/GQDs-10 %. This again proves that 5 % doping of GQDs was the best for electrochemical testing. Further increase in wt% ratio reduces the conductivity and increases the resistance due to blockage of active sites. Furthermore, the NSG-2composite provided an abundance of electroactive sites for electrolyte ion adsorption and promoted efficient charge transmission. Moreover, the homogeneous distribution of GQDs particles within the NiSrS increased the NiSrS/GQDs composites electrical conductivity. The NiSrS, NiSrS/GQD3%, NiSrS/GQD-5%, and NiSrS/GQD-10 % electrodes showed an equivalent series resistance (ESR) value of 1.315, 1.203, 1.045, and 1.147 U, respectively.

The CV of NiSrS, and NiSrS/GQDs composites were performed at various scan rates varying from 3 to 50 mV/s (Fig. 6 ). The difference in the voltage between the oxidation and reduction maxima is 0.37 V for the NiSrS-GQD-5% composite and NiSrS is 0.27V. The introduction of GQD into NiSrS resulted in lowering of peak separation than the pure NiSrS. This demonstrates a faster transfer rate for charge, indicating the higher performance of the composites. Also the redox reactions in the CV tests for all composites were the evidence of their battery type behavior. Furthermore, Fig. 6 shows that GQDs have a role in increasing the electrochemically active surface area, as evidenced by an improvement in the composite redox current when in contrast with a NiSrS electrode alone. The composite curves show uniformity in the redox peaks, indicating strong reversibility. Because of the limited duration for electrolyte ions to enter the electrodes innermost layers at much faster scan rates, curves shifted upwards.

GCD curves for the all composites were analyzed in Fig. 7 at multiple current densities ranging from 2 to 3.6 A/g. GCD curves additionally demonstrated the stronger electrochemically performance of the NiSrS/GQD-5% as compared to other NiSrS-GQDs composites. GQDs incorporation into the NiSrS material increases the electrochemically functional surface area, resulting a longer discharge time for the NiSrS/GQD-5% composite electrode. The nonlinear shape for GCD graphs indicated the diffusion controlled charge storage process in all NiSrS/GQDs composites.

Fig. 8 (a) showed a comparison of CV curves of NiSrS, NiSrS/GQD-3%, NiSrS/GQD-5%, and NiSrS/GQD-10 % composites. NiSrS/GQD-5% has a much larger region on the CV curve than NiSrS, NiSrS/GQD-3%, and NiSrS/GQD-10 %. This observation indicates an improvement in the composite electrochemical performance, which is demonstrated by subsequent observations of greater capacitance for NiSrS/GQD-5%. Fig. S3 represents the CV and GCD analysis for individual NiS and SrS.

Using equation (1), the capacity of all materials were derived from CV curves. Since the electrode has a pseudocapacitive charge-discharge curve, that there is no IR drop, that it is reversible, and that it has an incredible coulombic efficiency. Fig. 8(b) demonstrated the specific capacity of four electrodes, NiSrS, NiSrS-GQD-3%, NiSrS/GQD-5%, and NiSrS/GQD-10 % at various scan rate. The NiSrS/GQD-5% electrode had extraordinary specific capacity of 1829 C/g, overtaking pure NiSrS electrode that had a 523 C/g capacity (Fig. 8(c)). The NiSrS/GQD-3% and NiSrS/GQD-10 % showed 638 and 903 C/g capacity at 3 mV/s. The addition of GQDs to the NiSrS structure up to 5 % increased the combined specific capacitance of the material and improved its capacitance retention. But further increase of GQDs to 10 % reduces the electrochemical performance. This may be due to the fact that addition of GQDs hinders the active sites in the hetrostructure of NiSrS. Fig. 8(d) showed a comparison of GCD curves for NiSrS, NiSrS/GQD-3%, NiSrS/GQD-5%, and NiSrS/GQD-10 % electrode. As previously noted, doping of GQDs into the NiSrS structure simultaneously not only enhanced the electrical conductivity of the electrode but also formed additional electrochemically active surfaces, resulting in exceptional specific capacity. Equation (2) was utilized to find out the specific capacity from GCD test. The specific capacity (Cs) for NiSrS, NiSrS/GQD-3%, NiSrS/GQD-5%, and NiSrS/GQD-10 % electrode were depicted in Fig. 8(e). As can be observed at higher current densities the material can maintain sustainable capacity demonstrating their stable nature. The NiSrS, NiSrS/GQDs-3%, NiSrS/GQD-5%, and NiSrS/GQD-10 % electrode showed 540, 676, 1881, and 939 C/g capacity, respectively (Fig. 8(f)).

An asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device also known as supercapattery was developed utilizing the NSG-2 as the positive electrode along the activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode in a 1 M KOH electrolyte (Fig. 9 (a)). The potential window of NiSrS/GQD-5%//AC ASC was set from 0 to 1.8 V. The CV tests for NiSrS/GQD-5%//AC ASC device indicated double-layer capacitance together with pseudocapacitance. The NiSrS/GQD-5% (battery-type) and AC (capacitive type) components contribute the charge storage in the device. Furthermore, the CV curves did not alter significantly when scan rates increased, demonstrating high rate capacity.

In the investigation of the NiSrS/GQD-5%//AC ASC, the charge storage mechanism was extensively studied using the b-fitting model (Fig. 9(c)). By plotting the
log
v
against the
log
i p
and subsequently analyzing the output graph, the b-value was computed. This involved performing linear fitting to obtain the b-value. Remarkably, the b-value obtained for the NiSrS/GQD-5%//AC supercapattery ranged from 0.5 to 0.8, a range that falls within the recommended scope for supercapattery systems [58].

B-values within the 0.5–0.8 range are often associated with intertwined redox reactions and charge adsorption/desorption processes. Consequently, it can be inferred that charge storage in the NiSrS/GQD-5%//AC supercapattery stems from both redox reactions occurring at the NiSrS/GQD-5% anode and charge adsorption/desorption processes taking place at the AC cathode.

The symmetric pattern of the galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves were displayed in Fig. 9(d). The increasing current density revealed the reversibility of the electrochemical process and its greater columbic efficiency in the NiSrS/GQD-5%//AC ASC device.

Dunn's model was employed to further assess the storage participation in NiSrS/GQDs-5%//AC device [59,60].

In the equations mentioned, the capacitive and diffusive contributions are represented by k1 and k2, respectively. In Fig. 11 , the impact of increasing the scan rate on charge storage is illustrated. Dunn's model was applied to the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of the NiSrS/GQDs-5%//AC device at scan rates of 3, and 100 mV/s.

As the scan rate increases, the capacitive storage performance improves. This observation suggests that at lower scan rates, there is ample time for ions to interact with the electrode material, leading to enhanced capacitive contribution. Conversely, at higher scan rates, the available time for such ion-electrode interactions is limited, leading to a reduced diffusive contribution.

A conventional three-cell linear scan voltammogram has been utilized in an aqueous electrolyte to examine the electrocatalytic capacity of various NiSrS-based materials. The effectiveness of the synthesized nanostructures (NiSrS, NiSrS/GQDs-3%, NiSrS/GQDs-5%, and NiSrS/GQDs-10 %) was evaluated in contrast with a standard Pt/C control unit. The linear sweep polarization spectra of Pt/C, NiSrS, NiSrS/GQDs-3%, NiSrS/GQDs-5%, and NSG-3 in the alkaline medium are illustrated in Fig. 12 (a). It has been established that NSG-2outperform the other composites demonstrating an overpotential of 87 mV almost close to Pt/C (53 mV). As shown in Fig. 12(b), for obtaining a current density of 10 mA/cm2 in the alkaline solution, the synthesized composite electrocatalysts (NiSrS, NiSrS/GQDs-3%, and NiSrS/GQDs-10 %) required excessive potential of 235, 183, and 134 mV, respectively. A combination of factors including their lower overpotential values, NiSrS/NGQDs-5% demonstrate significantly improved HER performance than other composites. The increased intrinsic characteristics of the NiSrS/NGQDs-5% composite, including nanospheres with sharp expanding domains that are well-connected and organized, can be attributed to the enhanced electrocatalytic physical activity. Substantial interactions with the electrolyte and reliable electron transmission throughout the interface have been rendered attainable by these characteristics, which also give an enormous surface area and a number of active surfaces. Tafel lines that emerge from the polarization of Tafel curves (Fig. 12(c)) have been examined in order to further examine the characteristics of the HER electrocatalysts. NiSrS/NGQDs-5% demonstrated a Tafel slope that was identical to Pt/C (44.34 mV dec−1) and exhibited an immediate Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction (59.24 mV/dec). The Tafel slope for NiSrS (76.29 mV/dec), NiSrS/NGQDs-3% (69.28 mV/dec) and NiSrS/NGQDs-10 % (64.17 mV/dec) was higher than that for NiSrS/NGQDs-5%, indicating a slower reaction. The incredible electrocatalytic characteristics of the NiSrS/NGQDs-5% electrocatalyst in alkaline media are indicated by its low Tafel slope. The Tafel slope and overpotential of this investigation are contrasted with those of a prior investigation in Table 4 . The following reaction kinetics can be utilized in order to characterize the rate of the electrocatalytic reaction in an alkaline solution.

The HER results of NiSrS/NGQDs demonstrate that a metallic phase emerges and that broad-area interaction becomes attainable by the porous structure. A rapid exchange of ions and electrons between the electrolyte and composite electrocatalyst has been rendered achievable by the incorporation of NiSrS, which enhances HER activity. EIS examines were conducted to inquire into the HER electrocatalysts conductivity attributes. The EIS curves for the electrocatalysts NiSrS, NiSrS/GQDs-3%, NiSrS/GQDs-5%, and NSG-3were presented in Fig. 12(d). The decreased ohmic resistances (Rs) have been demonstrated by the enhanced interaction between NiSrS and GQDs. On the other hand, other two composites demonstrated deeper Rct and Rs values, which indicate rough exterior characteristics and the previously identified reduced HER performance of these materials. Additionally, the NiSrS/NGQDs composite exhibits more effective transmission of charge capabilities in electrolytes resulting from its immediate interactions between ions and electrodes and its mixed phase and the presence of active sites. The electrochemical impedance spectra illustrated in Fig. 12(d) demonstrate the actual transmission of charge and transportation process of NiSrS/NGQDs.

When examining the HER performance, durability is an essential factor. In an alkaline solution, chronoamperometry measurements (time vs current) were conducted continuously for 48 h. For the electrocatalysts consisting of NiSrS, NiSrS/GQDs-3%, NiSrS/GQDs-5%, and NiSrS/NGQDs-10 %, time is presented against the current in Fig. 12(e). These electrocatalysts demonstrate a dissimilar current density throughout the duration of the 40 h period, with an early increase accompanied by a linear decrease. The interaction of the surface and hydroxide ions may be partially responsible for this conduct. Additionally, the reduced accessibility of H+ ions, which interferes with HER reactions, induces a linear modification to the performance consistency. Again, NiSrS/NGQDs-5% perform better than the other two composites because it has a more stable structure. Fig. 12(f) represents the LSV curve for NSG-2before and after 5000 CV tests. This study illustrates the potential of heterostructures constructed from NiSrS/NGQDs in place of the further development of energy storage technologies and how they can be utilized in HER.

In conclusion, a high-performance supercapacitor electrode was developed by adopting a simple hydrothermal technique. NiSrS-GQD-5%, the resulting compound, had a three-dimensional structure that enhanced the number of active edges on the nanostructures. The optimized NiSrS/GQD-5% sample at 2.0 A/g had a high specific capacitance of 1881 C/g. Furthermore, when utilized in an asymmetric supercapacitors (NiSrS/GQDs-5%//AC), the energy density of 44 Wh/kg was achieved with a power density of 1750 W/kg, indicating its outstanding performance. Furthermore, after 10,000 cycles, the NiSrS/GQD-5%//AC device demonstrated unique 98.3 % cyclic stability and 100 % columbic efficiency, respectively. Furthermore, the NiSrS/GQDs composite demonstrated an insignificant overpotential of only 87 mV in the HER test. The results indicate that metal-doped nickel sulfide materials have interesting uses in high-performance supercapacitors.
